topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I was unpacking my desk drawer, a routine chore, when I saw it—a page from my private journal, subtly shifted in the corner of my roommate’s otherwise pristine textbook. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I slowly turned, my gaze locking onto Alex, who was nonchalantly flipping through a novel. His expression didn't change immediately, but the casual air around him suddenly felt thick, charged with something unwelcome. I walked over, my steps deliberate, and pointed a trembling finger at the page. "What is this?" My voice came out sharper than I intended, laced with a brittle edge. Alex looked up, a flicker of something unreadable in his eyes, before he quickly covered the page with his hand. The silence that followed was deafening, filled only by the frantic thumping of my own pulse.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked beneath a stack of graphic novels. My breath hitched; the air in the cramped room seemed to freeze solid. It was my handwriting, my deepest, most unguarded thoughts laid bare in my journal, and it was sitting open on Alex’s bedside table. A hot flush spread across my neck and cheeks. I didn't speak right away. Instead, I snatched the book, my fingers gripping the spine until my knuckles were white. I glared at Alex, my jaw tight enough to ache. "You shouldn't have looked at that," I managed, the words clipped and low, every syllable laced with sharp disapproval. He sighed, a sound that managed to be both weary and infuriatingly dismissive. He didn't meet my eyes, instead focusing intently on rearranging his laptop. The sheer audacity of the action made my hands shake slightly as I retreated to my own side of the room, needing space to process the sheer disrespect.

[story 3]

The realization dawned slowly, a cold dread creeping up my spine as I noticed the unfamiliar crease in the cover of my diary. It wasn't mine. It was open to a page detailing a conversation I’d only shared with myself. When I confronted Alex, the accusation hung heavy between us, suffocating the easy rhythm of our shared space. "I found this," I stated, my tone deliberately flat, devoid of warmth. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the tension radiating off me like heat. I watched his reaction closely, searching for any sign of apology or explanation, but he just leaned back against his pillows, crossing his arms over his chest. "It's just a book," he murmured, his voice smooth and annoyingly calm. The dismissiveness of his response made my throat tighten. I took a sharp, shallow breath, feeling the need to assert some boundary, even if my voice betrayed the sheer frustration boiling just beneath the surface.
